title: Pin<Box<ClientInner>> enforces C-pointer stability at the type level
description: client.rs: wh_Client_Init stores a raw pointer to the C context internally. If the Rust allocation moves after Init, that pointer becomes dangling. Pin<Box<ClientInner>> makes this a type-system guarantee rather than a convention. The comment in the source explains that Rust's declared-order field drop is also deliberately relied upon: client_ctx is declared before comm_cfg which is declared before transport, so Drop for Client can call wh_Client_Cleanup (which needs comm_cfg alive) before any field is dropped. A future contributor might suggest simplifying to Box<ClientInner> since 'we're careful not to move it'. That is wrong: Pin is the only guarantee that remains true under refactoring. Removing it would reintroduce a latent UB hazard that is invisible until a code path moves the box.
